title: Getting Started with Spring AI in Java
author: Asiff Mahmad
excerpt: Learn how to build AI-powered Java applications using Spring AI with OpenAI and other large language models.
content:
Getting Started with Spring AI in Java

Spring AI makes it easy to integrate large language models into Java applications. It provides abstractions for chat models, embeddings, image generation, and vector databases, allowing developers to switch between providers like OpenAI, Azure, and Anthropic with minimal code changes.

1. Setting Up Your Project

To get started, add the Spring AI milestone repository and the necessary dependencies to your pom.xml or build.gradle. You'll need the spring-boot-starter-web and the specific AI provider starter, for example, spring-ai-openai-spring-boot-starter.

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-ai-openai-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
  <version>1.0.0-M1</version>
</dependency>

2. Configuring the API Key

Security is paramount. Never hardcode your API keys. Instead, define them in your application.properties or application.yml, referencing environment variables:

spring.ai.openai.api-key=${OPENAI_API_KEY}

3. Creating Your First Chat Endpoint

With Spring AI, creating an endpoint that interacts with an LLM is incredibly straightforward. Inject the ChatClient into your REST controller and use it to generate responses.

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/chat")
public class ChatController {

    private final ChatClient chatClient;

    public ChatController(ChatClient.Builder chatClientBuilder) {
        this.chatClient = chatClientBuilder.build();
    }

    @GetMapping("/generate")
    public Map<String, String> generate(@RequestParam String message) {
        String response = chatClient.prompt()
            .user(message)
            .call()
            .content();
        return Map.of("generation", response);
    }
}

4. Next Steps in Prompt Engineering

Beyond simple text generation, Spring AI supports Prompt Templates, allowing you to create reusable, dynamic prompts with variables. This is the foundation for building complex workflows, summarizing text, or generating structured JSON output.

By abstracting the underlying API calls, Spring AI allows Java developers to focus on business logic rather than HTTP client boilerplate. In the next post, we will look at connecting this to a vector database for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

title: Building RAG Applications in Java
author: Asiff Mahmad
excerpt: Create Retrieval-Augmented Generation applications in Java using vector databases and Spring AI.
content:
Building RAG Applications in Java

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a powerful technique that combines the reasoning capabilities of large language models with your own private knowledge base. This prevents the model from hallucinating and allows it to answer questions about proprietary company data.

How RAG Works

The RAG architecture typically involves two phases:

  • Ingestion: Reading documents (PDFs, text files), chunking them into smaller pieces, converting those chunks into numerical vectors (embeddings), and storing them in a Vector Database.
  • Retrieval: Taking a user's query, converting it into a vector, searching the Vector Database for the most semantically similar chunks, and feeding those chunks to the LLM alongside the user's prompt.

Implementing RAG with Spring AI

Spring AI provides excellent abstractions for both the embedding process and the vector store integration. Here is a high-level overview of querying a vector database:

@Service
public class RagService {
    private final VectorStore vectorStore;
    private final ChatClient chatClient;

    public RagService(VectorStore vectorStore, ChatClient.Builder builder) {
        this.vectorStore = vectorStore;
        this.chatClient = builder.build();
    }

    public String askQuestion(String question) {
        // 1. Retrieve similar documents
        List<Document> similarDocuments = vectorStore.similaritySearch(SearchRequest.query(question).withTopK(3));
        
        // 2. Extract content
        String context = similarDocuments.stream()
            .map(Document::getContent)
            .collect(Collectors.joining("\n"));
            
        // 3. Ask the LLM with context
        String prompt = "Answer the question based only on the following context:\n" + context + "\n\nQuestion: " + question;
        return chatClient.prompt().user(prompt).call().content();
    }
}

Choosing a Vector Database

For Java applications, you have several excellent choices for vector databases. PgVector (PostgreSQL) is incredibly popular for teams already using relational databases. Alternatively, dedicated vector stores like Pinecone, Milvus, or ChromaDB offer extreme scalability for millions of vectors.

Implementing RAG transforms generic LLMs into highly specialized domain experts for your specific business needs.

title: Using LangChain4j for AI Development
author: Asiff Mahmad
excerpt: Explore LangChain4j and learn how to build intelligent Java applications with memory, tools, and AI agents.
content:
Using LangChain4j for AI Development

While Spring AI provides deep integration with the Spring ecosystem, LangChain4j offers a lightweight, highly expressive alternative that can run in any Java application, whether it's Spring Boot, Quarkus, or plain Java.

The Power of AI Services

LangChain4j introduces the concept of @AiService, which is arguably its most powerful feature. It allows you to define a Java interface, and LangChain4j automatically implements it using an LLM. It feels exactly like Spring Data JPA, but for AI.

public interface CustomerSupportAgent {
    @SystemMessage("You are a polite customer support agent. Keep answers concise.")
    String chat(String userMessage);
}

You then create the agent using the AiServices factory:

ChatLanguageModel model = OpenAiChatModel.builder()
    .apiKey(System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
    .modelName("gpt-4o-mini")
    .build();

CustomerSupportAgent agent = AiServices.create(CustomerSupportAgent.class, model);
String response = agent.chat("I want to return my recent order.");
System.out.println(response);

Adding Tools (Function Calling)

LLMs cannot access the internet or your database directly. LangChain4j solves this using Tools. You simply annotate a Java method with @Tool, and the LLM can decide when and how to call it during a conversation.

public class OrderService {
    @Tool("Gets the status of an order by its ID")
    public String getOrderStatus(String orderId) {
        // Database lookup logic here
        return "Order " + orderId + " is currently SHIPPED.";
    }
}

When you pass this OrderService instance to the AiServices factory, the LLM gains the ability to query order statuses autonomously. This is the foundation of building truly autonomous AI Agents in Java.

title: Deploying Java AI Applications with Docker
author: Asiff Mahmad
excerpt: Package your Java AI applications into Docker containers for scalable cloud deployment.
content:
Deploying Java AI Applications with Docker

Containerizing your AI-powered Java applications is essential for reliable deployment across different environments. In this post, we'll look at the best practices for writing a Dockerfile for a Spring Boot AI application.

The Dockerfile

We recommend using a multi-stage build. This allows you to compile the application inside a container (avoiding local Java version mismatches) and then copy only the compiled artifact into a lightweight runtime image (like Eclipse Temurin or Alpine).

# Stage 1: Build
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY mvnw .
COPY .mvn .mvn
COPY pom.xml .
RUN ./mvnw dependency:go-offline
COPY src src
RUN ./mvnw package -DskipTests

# Stage 2: Run
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/target/*.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]

Handling API Keys Securely

Never commit your OPENAI_API_KEY or database credentials into your Docker image. Instead, pass them as environment variables at runtime.

docker run -d -p 8080:8080 \
  -e OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-your-secret-key \
  -e SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod \
  my-java-ai-app:latest

Optimizing Startup Time

If your AI application needs to scale rapidly (e.g., in response to a surge in chat requests), standard JVM startup times might be a bottleneck. Consider compiling your Spring Boot application to a GraalVM Native Image. This can reduce startup time from seconds to milliseconds, making it ideal for Serverless AI deployments on platforms like AWS Lambda or Google Cloud Run.

title: Building AI REST APIs with Spring Boot
author: Asiff Mahmad
excerpt: Create scalable REST APIs that leverage large language models using Spring Boot.
content:
Building AI REST APIs with Spring Boot

REST APIs remain the foundation of enterprise Java. This guide demonstrates how to expose AI capabilities through REST endpoints.

Key topics include request validation, handling rate limits from AI providers, streaming responses using Server-Sent Events (SSE), and ensuring high availability for your production AI endpoints.

title: Vector Databases for Java Developers
author: Asiff Mahmad
excerpt: Understand embeddings, semantic search, and vector databases in modern Java AI applications.
content:
Vector Databases for Java Developers

Vector databases store numerical representations of text called embeddings. These databases power chatbots, recommendation engines, and enterprise search solutions.

We explore how to use Pinecone, Milvus, Weaviate, Qdrant, and Chroma directly from Java, covering similarity search, indexing strategies, metadata filtering, and hybrid search techniques.

title: Integrating OpenAI APIs in Spring Boot
author: Asiff Mahmad
excerpt: Learn multiple approaches for integrating OpenAI models into your Spring Boot applications.
content:
Integrating OpenAI APIs in Spring Boot

Spring Boot offers several ways to connect with OpenAI. This tutorial compares using RestTemplate, WebClient, Spring AI, and Reactive APIs.

You'll learn secure API key management, request handling, retries, and response parsing for production workloads.
